person.headline: Cybersecurity leader bridging military discipline with enterprise security strategy
person.summary: Eight years of Army service in signal and cyber operations followed by a decade in private-sector cybersecurity. Led SOC teams, built zero-trust architectures, and earned CISSP and CISM certifications. Comfortable briefing generals and board members alike.

workAuthorization.renderAs: Authorized to work in the US; no sponsorship required.

competencies.label: Security Operations & Incident Response
competencies.description: Built and led SOC teams from scratch in both military and corporate settings. Defined playbooks, triage workflows, and escalation procedures. Responded to nation-state intrusions and ransomware events.

competencies.label: Security Architecture & Zero Trust
competencies.description: Designed zero-trust network architectures for hybrid cloud environments. Led FedRAMP authorization for a SaaS product. Translated military DISA STIG experience into enterprise hardening standards.

competencies.label: Leadership & Cross-Functional Communication
competencies.description: Led teams of 8-40 across military and civilian contexts. Briefed flag officers, C-suite executives, and board audit committees on risk posture and incident status. Mentored junior analysts through career transitions.

army.mil.name: United States Army
army.mil.description: The land warfare branch of the U.S. Armed Forces. Largest branch by active-duty headcount.

fortgordon.army.mil.name: U.S. Army Cyber Center of Excellence
fortgordon.army.mil.description: Signal and cyber training center at Fort Gordon (renamed Fort Eisenhower in 2023), GA. Trains all Army signal and cyber MOS fields.

meridianhealth.example.com.name: Meridian Health Systems
meridianhealth.example.com.description: Mid-market healthcare IT company providing EHR and patient portal platforms to regional hospital networks. Private equity-backed.

vetsintech.org.name: VetsinTech
vetsintech.org.description: National nonprofit helping military veterans transition into technology careers through training, networking, and mentorship programs.

utsa.edu.description: Public research university with a nationally recognized cybersecurity program. NSA/DHS designated Center of Academic Excellence in Cyber Defense.

aegiscyber.example.com.name: Aegis Cyber Defense
aegiscyber.example.com.description: Endpoint security and incident response firm. Known for hands-on IR engagements against advanced persistent threats.

tidewaterfederal.example.com.description: Defense and federal IT consulting firm supporting DoD and intelligence community cybersecurity programs.

positions.title: Director of Cybersecurity
positions.summary: Head of cybersecurity for a healthcare IT company handling PHI for 2M+ patients. Report to CISO. Built the security operations function and led the company through SOC 2 Type II and HITRUST certification.

achievements.statement: Built SOC team from 0 to 12 analysts, achieving 24/7 coverage within 6 months

attribution.scope: Owned SOC buildout plan, hiring model, coverage target, and operating cadence; direct managers and team leads handled some day-to-day execution as the team scaled.
attribution.notes: A future review should clarify which parts Maria personally managed versus delegated through leads once the team reached 12 analysts.

achievements.statement: Led HITRUST r2 certification, passing on first assessment with zero corrective actions
Reduced mean time to detect (MTTD) from 72 hours to under 4 hours through SIEM tuning and automated triage

Led response to a hospital-wide ransomware incident — performed forensic analysis on the attacker tooling, advised leadership against paying the ransom based on observed decryption failures in adjacent engagements, and executed an alternate recovery path from offline backups; restored critical clinical systems within 41 hours with zero patient-care impact.
achievements.shortStatement: Led ransomware response and recommended against paying based on attacker-tooling analysis; restored systems from backups in 41 hours.
achievements.longform:
On a Friday evening, ransomware encrypted a significant portion of Meridian's EHR-adjacent infrastructure. Hospital leadership's first instinct was to pay quickly to restore operations — patient care was at stake, and the demand (in the low seven figures) was small relative to estimated downtime costs.

I personally led the forensic analysis on the dropped tooling. Indicators tied the activity to a family whose decryptor had a documented failure rate of ~30% in adjacent engagements I had visibility into — paying carried real risk of getting the decryption key and still losing material data. I argued the case to the CFO and the CEO with the evidence, recommended we proceed with backup restoration, and committed to a 48-hour timeline. We brought critical clinical systems back in 41 hours with no patient-care impact and no payment.

Two lessons I take from this. First, the value of having walked the forensic detail myself — leadership would not have accepted a 'paying is risky' framing from a layer of indirection; they accepted it when I could describe specifically why the attacker's decryptor failed in observed cases. Second, the importance of pre-committing to a timeline. The 48-hour commitment turned the conversation from 'should we pay' to 'can we execute' — and gave the team something concrete to drive to.

I also note: the decision could have gone the wrong way. If our backups had been compromised (we checked) or if our restoration runbooks had failed to hold up, the cost would have been a career and a hospital's trust. This story is recorded here in full because the nuance matters: leadership judgment that turns out right is not the same as the same judgment turning out wrong. The forensic basis was what made it the right call.

metrics.note: time-to-restore: critical clinical systems back online from offline backups
patient_care_impact: zero patient-care incidents attributed to the outage
ransom_paid: no ransom was paid; recommendation against payment was accepted by leadership

provenance.note: Mined through targeted probing during a conversation about a CISO-track role posting. The original resume bullet point under this position read 'Led response to a ransomware incident and restored critical clinical systems within 41 hours with zero patient-care impact.' The conversation surfaced the specific judgment call, the forensic basis, and the lessons. The longform deliberately captures both what went right and the counterfactual (it could have gone wrong), which interview prep needs but a resume does not.

narrativeVariants.label: Public resume bullet point
narrativeVariants.statement: Led ransomware response that restored critical clinical systems from offline backups within 41 hours with no patient-care impact.
narrativeVariants.notes: Public-safe version omits the ransom-payment debate, attacker-family detail, and internal executive-room context.

narrativeVariants.label: Interview-prep framing
narrativeVariants.longform: Use this story to show executive judgment under pressure: Maria did the forensic analysis herself, explained the risk of paying in business terms, committed to a 48-hour recovery path, and owned the recommendation when the outcome was uncertain.
narrativeVariants.notes: Useful for preparing the story, not for direct resume output.

narrativeVariants.label: Security leadership framing
narrativeVariants.statement: Led evidence-based ransomware recovery by analyzing attacker tooling, advising executives against payment, and restoring critical systems from backups within 41 hours.
narrativeVariants.notes: Emphasizes incident-response judgment and executive risk communication for security leadership audiences.

narrativeVariants.label: Healthcare security framing
narrativeVariants.statement: Protected patient-care continuity during a ransomware event by leading evidence-based recovery from offline backups and restoring critical clinical systems within 41 hours.
narrativeVariants.notes: Emphasizes care-continuity impact for healthcare audiences without naming the affected clinical system.

achievements.statement: Managed transition from an external MSSP to an in-house tier-1 alert triage capability during a 30% security budget reduction; maintained 24/7 coverage and improved alert quality through tuning.

provenance.note: Keep this in the master because it is useful for ops-leadership, budget-stewardship, and cost-management audiences. Usually omit it from CISO-track or strategic-leadership resumes unless the target role specifically values efficiency work; stronger executive-judgment stories should take priority when space is tight.

Self-assessed during Topgrading-style prep. She would likely rate me 9 — strong on incident response and SOC scaling, slightly less strong on board-level communication, which is the area we'd both flag for development.

reflections.text: The ransomware response. Most of my proudest moments in this role were team accomplishments, but the moment that taught me the most about leadership was sitting in the executive conference room on a Friday night, walking the CEO and CFO through why I thought paying the ransom would fail. I had to make the technical case in language they could verify, commit to a timeline, and own the outcome. The team did the recovery work; my contribution was the analysis and the commitment. Looking back, I'm proudest that I did the forensic work myself instead of delegating it — leadership wouldn't have accepted a derivative argument.

provenance.note: This reflection seeded the mhs-ransomware-2024 achievement entry. The raw voice is preserved here; the structured achievement is in achievements. Future conversations can probe further (e.g. 'what would you have done if backups had failed') without re-eliciting the core memory.

reflections.text: Underinvesting in the security awareness program in the first nine months. I prioritized technical controls because the SOC was being built, but the phishing click-through rate stayed stubbornly above 20% well into year two. I should have run a parallel awareness track from the start — it took us 18 months to get below 8%, and the early window was wasted. The lesson is that technical controls and human factors have different time-to-impact curves, and the right move with a fresh budget is to fund both early rather than sequentially.

provenance.note: Not yet distilled into a structured achievement — this is a candidate for mining in a future conversation. There's likely a phishing-rate-reduction achievement (20%+ → <8%) that should exist with metrics, but the framing for the resume version isn't obvious yet because it has to lead with the lesson rather than the win. Left as a reflection for now.

reflections.text: My first month at Meridian, I asked an analyst to cover a weekend shift and he just said no. In the Army I never heard 'no' to a lawful tasking. I'd been a civilian five years by then — but as an analyst, then a consultant. Meridian was the first time since the Army I had people to task. I sat in my car in the parking garage for a while after that one. What I eventually understood is that nobody in that building had ever watched me earn anything. In the Army, my rank was the authority. Out here, working for someone meant something different. So I stopped asking for anything I hadn't done first. I took the worst on-call rotations for two months and wrote up every handoff like it mattered, because it did. By the time we staffed the overnight shift, I had a waiting list to get on my team. The Army would have called that bad delegation. I call it the thing that worked.

provenance.note: Preserved verbatim from the prompt: 'Tell me one story about your work that you would never put on a formal resume.' This seeded the authority-from-demonstrated-work talking point.

reflections.text: Yes, if the operational maturity were two years further along than it was when I joined. Building from greenfield is energizing for a tour; a second tour would want to be a build-and-scale or a turnaround, not another start-from-zero.

positions.title: Senior Incident Response Consultant
positions.summary: Frontline IR consultant on Aegis Cyber Defense's services team. Investigated breaches for Fortune 500 clients across healthcare, financial services, and manufacturing.

achievements.statement: Led incident response for 15+ engagements including 3 nation-state intrusions and 2 ransomware events affecting critical infrastructure
Developed automated forensic triage toolkit adopted by the broader IR team, reducing initial assessment time by 40%

progression.note: Lateral move into leadership role at Meridian

positions.title: Cybersecurity Analyst
positions.summary: DoD contract supporting cyber threat analysis. First civilian role after Army separation. Applied military cyber experience to defense contractor environment.

achievements.statement: Performed threat analysis supporting DoD network defense operations, authoring 50+ threat intelligence reports

positions.title: Cyber Operations Specialist
positions.summary: Led a 6-person cyber operations team conducting defensive cyber operations and vulnerability assessments for Army networks.

achievements.statement: Led defensive cyber operations team protecting 15,000-node enterprise network, maintaining zero successful intrusions during 18-month tenure

narrativeVariants.notes: Federal and defense audiences expect rank and MOS; omitting them can read as concealment, not brevity.

achievements.statement: Trained and mentored 12 junior soldiers transitioning into the new 17C MOS career field

deployments.description: Deployed as part of ARCYBER forward element supporting OIR cyber operations.

positions.summary: Maintained and operated signal systems supporting brigade-level communications. Progressed from Specialist to Sergeant.

achievements.statement: Maintained 99.7% uptime on tactical communications systems supporting a 3,500-soldier brigade

deployments.name: Operation Enduring Freedom
deployments.description: 11-month deployment providing signal support to brigade combat team.

progression.note: Entered as PV2 (E-2) Aug 2010; PFC (E-3) 2011; SPC (E-4) 2012; SGT (E-5) 2014; SSG (E-6) Mar 2017. Reclassified from 25U to 17C in Jan 2016 when the Army stood up the cyber MOS. Honorably discharged Aug 2018 as SSG. Final rank appears on the source resume; the dated progression was established during the fictional 2026-06-11 military record pass so the example's rank, MOS, and leadership claims form a coherent story.

spanning.statement: Progressed from Private (E-2) to Staff Sergeant (E-6) across eight years of service, with four promotions.

supportingFacts.statement: Reclassified from 25U (Signal Support) to 17C (Cyber Operations) when the Army stood up the cyber MOS

provenance.note: Final rank and MOS appear on the source resume; the dated steps were established during Conversation Six.

spanning.statement: Army Commendation Medal (x2), Army Achievement Medal (x3)
Held TS/SCI clearance throughout service with CI polygraph

exitContext.statement: Completed service obligation and transitioned to defense contracting to stay in the cyber field with better pay.

education.institution: University of Texas at San Antonio

achievements.statement: Thesis: 'Automated Threat Hunting in Zero Trust Environments Using Machine Learning'
Completed degree while serving active duty, using military tuition assistance

service.description: Mentor transitioning veterans through tech career workshops and resume reviews. Lead the San Antonio chapter's cybersecurity track.

speaking.title: From MOS to SOC: Building Cyber Careers After Service
speaking.description: Keynote on military-to-civilian cyber career transitions.

references.relationship: Battalion Commander at Fort Gordon, oversaw cyber operations team
references.title: VP of Cyber Programs, Raytheon
references.notes: Prefers phone. Very supportive of my transition.

interests.name: Capture The Flag (CTF) competitions
interests.description: Active competitor on DefCon CTF team 'VetSec'. Placed top 20 in 2024.

interests.name: Youth STEM mentoring
interests.description: Volunteer instructor for CyberPatriot, Air Force Association's youth cyber education program.

goals.summary: Build or lead a security organization at an established company, ideally a CISO or VP Security role. Strong second preference: lead a focused security capability (SOC, IR, security engineering) at a fast-growing pre-IPO company where there's room to shape function from the ground up.

roles.title: CISO
roles.notes: Established companies preferred to true greenfield startups; want to inherit something rather than build from zero again.

roles.title: Head of SOC / IR
roles.notes: Open to functional leadership at larger scale if title is right.

constraints: No on-call rotation as a primary contributor — willing to be the executive escalation for major incidents
No companies under 50 employees — want operating scale, not a true zero-to-one build
motivations: Lead a function at organizational scale rather than continuing to be a player-coach builder
Sit at the executive table on security strategy rather than translate it up
Mentor a deeper security bench than I have today

cautions.claim: characterized as a Big Four consulting alumnus
cautions.reason: Tidewater Federal is a defense consulting firm with similar work but a different brand bucket. Some recruiters group them together; the user prefers to be positioned against defense consulting peers, not Deloitte / EY / KPMG / PwC.

cautions.claim: claimed as an AI / ML security specialist
cautions.reason: Has good operational exposure to ML-based detection tooling but does not have research-level expertise. Past LLM draft positioned this too strongly; corrected here.

provenance.note: Captured after an earlier draft over-positioned ML expertise; the user pushed back during review.

cautions.claim: described as having held a leadership rank throughout her Army service
cautions.reason: Maria was junior enlisted (E-2 to E-4) from 2010-2014. Leadership claims apply to the SGT/SSG years from 2014-2018. Anyone who served reads rank against time instantly; the progression must support the verb.

provenance.note: Captured during a military record pass that made the promotion timeline behind the source resume's final rank internally coherent.

openQuestions.question: Mine the biggest-mistake reflection (security awareness underinvestment) into a structured achievement — the frame should lead with the lesson, not the win, and that framing isn't obvious yet.
openQuestions.context: Surfaced during reflections elicitation; left as a reflection for now.

openQuestions.question: Clarify whether the ransomware-response achievement should name the affected clinical system or keep the description generic.
openQuestions.context: The current achievement is strong, but more specificity may improve interview storytelling while also increasing sensitivity. Decide during review before using it in external materials.

openQuestions.question: Explore whether Maria has additional civilian-sector examples of handling hard staffing issues, such as coaching, performance plans, reassignments, or managing out poor performers, after moving from military leadership into healthcare security leadership.
openQuestions.context: Her first-month Meridian story now gives story-backed material for the military-to-civilian leadership transition: she learned that civilian authority had to be rebuilt through demonstrated work rather than inherited rank. This question should now look for additional evidence beyond the on-call staffing story, especially harder performance-management examples that could become either private reflections or carefully framed leadership achievements.

provenance.note: Added during a later review pass that looked for leadership gaps not obvious from the source resume.

openQuestions.question: For the SOC buildout, clarify what Maria directly owned versus what her managers or team leads owned as the team scaled from 0 to 12 analysts.
openQuestions.context: The achievement is strong, but attribution precision will help curators choose honest verbs. Explore budget ownership, hiring authority, headcount responsibility, and who reported progress upward.

provenance.note: Added after attribution fields were introduced to prompt a cleaner distinction between owned, led, and delegated work.

voice.customNotes: Active voice, past tense for completed work. Short sentences over compound ones. When in doubt between two framings, pick the one that names what was actually done rather than what was 'contributed to'.

aiInstructions.text: Push back when I undersell. Default to plain-direct voice. When drafting for a CISO-track role, prioritize executive-judgment achievements over technical-depth achievements unless the JD specifically calls for deep technical hands-on work. When unsure about something I might have said in a previous session, ask before drafting — do not pattern-match from generic security-leader resumes I have not approved.

talkingPoints.label: Authority from demonstrated work
talkingPoints.statement: I rebuild authority from demonstrated work rather than inherited position.

supportingEvidence.summary: First-month Meridian story about rebuilding authority after an analyst declined a weekend shift.
Built SOC from 0 to 12 analysts and reached 24/7 coverage within six months.

talkingPoints.evidenceSummary: Supported by Maria's first-month Meridian story and by the SOC buildout that reached 24/7 coverage in six months.

provenance.note: Offered as a hypothesis after preserving Maria's raw story: 'Does this ring true — you rebuild authority from demonstrated work rather than inherited position?' Maria confirmed the framing.
